metadata.raw_response_text:
**Give the Bus a Fighting Chance**

On any congested urban corridor, the pattern is familiar: a long line of cars inching forward, horns blaring, tempers rising, and somewhere in the middle of it all, a bus packed with dozens of people forced to crawl at the same miserable pace as single-occupancy vehicles. It is one of the clearest signs of a transportation system that is failing to match street space with public need. If cities are serious about moving more people, reducing delays, and making urban life more workable, they should install dedicated bus lanes on their busiest corridors.

This is not a radical idea. It is a practical one.

A single bus can carry far more people than a handful of private cars occupying the same amount of road space. Yet on many major streets, buses are treated as just another vehicle, trapped in traffic despite being the most space-efficient mode on the road. That makes little sense. When a city gives the same priority to a bus carrying 50 people as it gives to a car carrying one or two, it is effectively choosing inefficiency.

Dedicated bus lanes correct that imbalance. They allow transit to move reliably even when traffic is heavy. That reliability matters as much as speed. Riders can tolerate a trip that takes 20 minutes if it consistently takes 20 minutes. What they cannot plan around is a trip that sometimes takes 20 minutes and sometimes 45 because buses are stuck behind turning cars, delivery trucks, and congestion. Dedicated lanes make transit predictable, and predictable transit is usable transit.

That matters not just for current riders, but for everyone else on the corridor. When buses become faster and more dependable, more people choose them. Some drivers leave their cars at home. That shift reduces overall traffic pressure, which benefits those who still need to drive: delivery vehicles, emergency services, tradespeople, and people with mobility needs. A dedicated bus lane is often attacked as “taking away” road space, but in reality it is a way of using scarce road space more intelligently.

Critics often frame the issue as a war on drivers. It is nothing of the sort. The real question is simple: on a corridor where space is limited, should policy reward the least efficient use of that space, or the most efficient? If one lane can move several times more people by bus than by private car, then dedicating that lane to buses is not ideological. It is common sense.

There is also a basic fairness argument. Bus riders are often working people, students, seniors, and lower-income residents who have the least flexibility in their schedules and the fewest transportation alternatives. They should not be asked to bear the worst delays simply because they chose—or could only afford—the most efficient mode available. A city that leaves bus riders sitting in traffic while protecting every inch of road for cars is making a social choice, and not a very defensible one.

Environmental concerns strengthen the case. Cities cannot talk seriously about cutting emissions while allowing buses to idle endlessly in congestion. Faster buses mean more efficient operations, lower fuel use, and stronger transit ridership. Even before full electrification, bus priority is a climate policy. It is also a public health policy: fewer cars, less stop-and-go traffic, and cleaner air on corridors that often run through dense neighborhoods.

Businesses sometimes worry that dedicated bus lanes will hurt commerce by reducing parking or slowing customer access. But thriving urban business districts depend on people, not just cars. Bus riders are customers too, and in many corridors they are the majority of the people passing through every day. A street that moves large numbers of people efficiently is better for shops and restaurants than one that is permanently gridlocked. Congestion does not create vitality; it creates frustration.

Of course, bus lanes must be designed well. They should be paired with clear enforcement, sensible loading zones, safe pedestrian crossings, and boarding improvements that keep buses moving. If cities paint a lane red and then allow it to be blocked by double-parked vehicles all day, they will undermine their own policy. But bad implementation is an argument for better execution, not for abandoning the concept.

The strongest case for dedicated bus lanes is that they acknowledge a truth cities can no longer avoid: not everyone can drive everywhere, and no major urban corridor can function well if every traveler tries to use a private car. Streets are finite. Demand is not. The only way to prevent permanent paralysis is to prioritize modes that move the greatest number of people in the least amount of space.

Dedicated bus lanes do exactly that.

A congested corridor is already telling city leaders something important. It is saying the old arrangement is not working. The answer is not to accept delay as inevitable or to keep all vehicles equally stuck. The answer is to favor the mode that offers the broadest public benefit. If a bus carries more people, creates less pollution, and gives working residents a dependable way to get around, then it deserves more than a place in traffic.

It deserves its own lane.
